county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873 (reorganized 1909 when Rosebud Reservation lands were opened). Named for Bartlett Tripp, chief justice of Dakota Territory. Winner is the largest town.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tripp County Register of Deeds in Winner holds deed records; effectively organized in 1909 when former Rosebud Reservation lands were opened. BLM GLO homestead records document settlers who filed claims on opened reservation lands. The 1909 Tripp County land lottery drew thousands — lottery registration records at SDSHS identify claimants. Tripp County Clerk of Courts has probate, naturalization, and civil records. The Rosebud Sioux Reservation borders the county — BIA records at NARA Kansas City include census rolls for Sicangu Lakota families. The Winner Advocate newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Czech, German, and Scandinavian settlers were among the successful lottery winners. Catholic and Lutheran church records are important sources.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1873 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1873. Register of Deeds issued licenses; state registration began 1905.

source_profiles.source_name: Tripp County Birth and Death Records
source_profiles.repository: South Dakota Vital Records, Pierre / Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State registration from 1905; county-filed records may predate statewide compliance. Certified copies from SD Department of Health Vital Records. Some counties have earlier records filed with the Register of Deeds.

source_profiles.source_name: Tripp County Clerk of Courts Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate, guardianship, adoption, naturalization, civil and criminal case files from 1873. Clerk of Courts maintains court records for the circuit court.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Tripp County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Tripp County including homestead, preemption, timber culture, and cash entries. South Dakota was entirely public domain — GLO records are the primary source for first land ownership.
Federal population schedules for Tripp County. Dakota Territory enumerated from 1860. Agricultural schedules (1850-1880) provide additional family and economic data.

source_profiles.repository: South Dakota State Historical Society / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers from Tripp County communities. SDSHS holds the largest collection of SD newspapers on microfilm. Chronicling America has digitized SD papers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1871. Named for John W. Turner, Dakota Territory legislator. One of the earlier-organized counties in southeastern SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Turner County Register of Deeds in Parker holds deed records from 1871 — among SD's older county records. German-Russian (Mennonite and Hutterite) and Scandinavian settlers predominated. BLM GLO homestead records document original claims. Turner County Clerk of Courts in Parker has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Mennonite church records at the Center for Mennonite Brethren Studies and Mennonite Library and Archives are essential for the German-Russian Mennonite community. Hutterite colonies maintain their own records. The Parker New Era and Marion Record newspapers are on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The county was settled early and has strong German-Russian cultural heritage. Lutheran church records serve the Scandinavian families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1862 as original Dakota Territory county. Named for the Union (Civil War era). Located at the extreme southeastern corner of SD at the confluence of the Big Sioux and Missouri Rivers. Oldest organized county in SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Union County Register of Deeds in Elk Point holds some of the oldest county land records in SD from 1862. As an original Dakota Territory county and possibly the first organized county in SD, territorial-era records may also be at SDSHS. Lewis and Clark passed through in 1804; the Spirit Mound area was noted in their journals. BLM GLO homestead records cover original claims. Union County Clerk of Courts has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Scandinavian and Anglo-American settlers were among the earliest in Dakota Territory. The Elk Point Leader newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Missouri River and Big Sioux River confluence made Union County an early point of entry for settlers. The county's position on the Iowa and Nebraska borders means cross-state research is often necessary.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873. Named for Walworth County, Wisconsin. Located along the Missouri River in north-central SD. Contains portions of Standing Rock and Cheyenne River Reservations.
county_metadata.research_tips: Walworth County Register of Deeds in Selby holds deed records from 1873. The county borders Standing Rock and Cheyenne River Reservations — BIA records at NARA Kansas City include census rolls and allotment records for Lakota and Dakota families. Mobridge (though in Walworth County) was a major railroad town and Missouri River crossing. BLM GLO homestead records document settlers. Walworth County Clerk of Courts in Selby has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Oahe Dam (1948-1962) impacted Missouri River communities; pre-dam records document displaced families. German-Russian and Scandinavian settlers predominated. The Selby Record and Mobridge Tribune newspapers are on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Sitting Bull's burial site is near Mobridge.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1862 as original Dakota Territory county. Yankton was the first capital of Dakota Territory (1861-1883). Named for the Yankton Sioux. Contains Yankton Sioux Reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yankton County Register of Deeds in Yankton holds some of SD's oldest and most important land records from 1862. Yankton was the first capital of Dakota Territory (1861-1883); territorial government records at SDSHS are essential for early research. The Yankton Sioux Reservation is within the county — BIA records at NARA Kansas City include census rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence for Yankton Sioux (Ihanktonwan Dakota) families. The Mead Cultural Education Center in Yankton has local genealogy collections. BLM GLO homestead records cover original claims. Yankton County Clerk of Courts has extensive probate, naturalization, and civil records from the territorial era. The Yankton Press and Dakotan newspaper (1861+) is one of SD's oldest newspapers, on microfilm at SDSHS. Czech and German settlers were significant. Mount Marty College (Catholic, 1936) records document Catholic families. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1862 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1911. Contains large portion of Cheyenne River Indian Reservation. Named for Frank Ziebach, SD newspaper editor and legislator. One of SD's least populated counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ziebach County Register of Deeds in Dupree holds records from 1911. The county is largely within Cheyenne River Indian Reservation — BIA records at NARA Kansas City are essential, including Indian census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, school records (Cheyenne River schools), and agency correspondence for Minneconjou, Two Kettle, Sans Arc, and Blackfoot Lakota bands. BLM GLO homestead records document settlers who filed on opened reservation lands. Ziebach County Clerk of Courts has probate and civil records. Tribal enrollment records at Cheyenne River Agency document Lakota families. The Dupree Leader newspaper at SDSHS covers community history. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed county records. Ranching is the primary economy. Fred Dupree, who preserved a buffalo herd from near-extinction, is associated with the county.
